stories.title: The Thief-Mage's First Heist
stories.text:
Kael Stormwind was twenty-three summers old when he stole the Lantern of Morath from the vault beneath Pelimere's Counting House. He was a novice thief with untrained talent for the Art—a street-mage's bastard who'd learned magic through whispered kitchen gossip and desperate improvisation. The Lantern burned with old light, and the magistrates guarded it like a crown jewel. But Kael needed it to save his sister's life, and need is a better teacher than fear.

The Counting House squatted in the Merchant Quarter, its limestone facade carved with record-ledgers. Kael spent three weeks posing as a scribe's apprentice, mapping the vault-keeper's rhythms. At midnight on a new moon—when shadow-magic runs thin but illusions hold strongest—he cast his first true working: a spell of seeming that painted him invisible to lamplight and watchful eyes. His heart hammered. The magic cost him a day's memory—he'd wake tomorrow having lost August's third week—but the price was worth paying.

He descended through two locked doors his fencing-master's sister had copied in wax. Below lay the deep vault, where the Lantern hung suspended in an iron cradle. Kael's fingers trembled as he reached for it. The moment his skin touched cold metal, the spell shattered. Wards shrieked. Bells tolled across the city.

But Kael was already running. He'd memorized a second route—through the scribal cellars, beneath the market-stalls, and out into the Saltmere Canal where a smuggler named Missa waited with her shallow-draft boat. His legs burned. His lungs screamed. Behind him, guards shouted and torch-flame flickered.

Missa hauled him aboard as crossbow bolts whistled overhead. The Lantern—warm against his chest—blazed like a second sun as the boat kicked into the current, speeding south toward the harbor and open water. For the first time, Kael laughed—wild, terrified, triumphant. He'd done it. He'd stolen the impossible.

And tomorrow, when he woke with no memory of August's third week, he would carry the Lantern to the apothecary in the Drowned Quarter, trade it for the poison-cure only that merchant kept, and save his sister's life. Tonight, though, he was just a thief-mage flying across dark water, alive with the certain knowledge that he was capable of more than he'd ever imagined.

stories.title: The Ledger-Thief's Confession
stories.text: In the shadow-markets of Vethrand, a forger named Calix discovers that the phantom-ledgers he's been counterfeiting are *responding* to him—updating themselves with truths he never wrote. Each new entry pulls memories from refugees he's never met, refugees who vanished in the Burned Expanse. When Sevin the Auditor finds him, Calix realizes he's been unknowingly channeling Liv's fractured archive through his fingertips. The choice: destroy the phantom-ledgers and sever the archive's voice, or let them continue singing contradictions into reality. But something worse lurks in the margins—the Tower's hunger is following the ledgers' frequency, hunting them across three provinces. Calix must decide whether to hide his creations or weaponize them against the very forces consuming the world's records.

stories.title: The Bone-Singer's Defection
stories.text: Dren, a junior bone-singer trained in the old ways, discovers that Kess of the Drowned Hollows has been teaching the transformed merchants not just Elder Language, but fragments of a pre-human summoning-song. The consequence: the pre-human dead are *withdrawing* from the covenant entirely, preparing to emerge without negotiation. Dren is ordered by her circle-keeper to sever Kess's connection to the Singing Catacombs—a working that would shatter Kess's mind but preserve the boundary. Instead, Dren defects, stealing bone-carved keys that unlock the catacombs' deepest chambers. What she finds there is not rescue—it's an archive older than the Dominion, one that reveals the pre-human dead *want* to return, and Kess has been facilitating their resurrection all along. Now hunted by her own order, Dren must decide: help complete what Kess started, or find a third path that doesn't end in drowning.

stories.title: The Cartographer's Blind Map
stories.text: Aldrin, Mardoc's apprentice, discovers a map in her master's dissolving papers—one that doesn't exist on any waking surface. It shows the Tower's hunger made visible: ley-lines are being *consumed* from inside-out, leaving voids where roads used to be. Worse, the map is updating in real-time, and Aldrin can see her own position on it, which means the Tower can see her. She flees to Pale Crossing, seeking Liv's archive, hoping the refugee testimonies might contain counter-maps or blind-spots. But the Tower's cartography follows her through the very act of *being mapped*—every town she enters shifts on the page, every path she takes rewrites itself. Aldrin must choose between destroying the map (and losing the only warning of the Tower's spread) or learning to lie to cartography itself, to walk unmapped ways that should not exist.

stories.title: The Shade-Collector's Unraveling
stories.text: Vex, a merchant who traded in rare luminescence, discovers that the objects she hoarded are *bleeding their essence* into the world around her. Her warehouse has become a site of spontaneous transformation—inanimate goods developing hunger, desire, strange agency. A scholar named Teren approaches with a theory: Vex's collection is resonating with fragments of Zareth scattered across the realm, and the fragments are using her inventory as a beacon to reassemble. Each object she's collected now carries a piece of the cosmic entity, and they're organizing themselves toward reunion. The Empire's Auditors close in, wanting her collection for study. Vex must decide whether to scatter her hoard across the realm to prevent Zareth's reassembly, or find a way to control the fragmentation before the entire region destabilizes into something neither real nor imagined.

stories.title: The Refuge-Keeper's Gambit
stories.text: At Pale Crossing waystation, a caravan master named Dorn begins rationing water as the Burned Expanse's shortages spread inland. But refugees arriving speak of waystation *paradoxes*—places where two versions of Pale Crossing exist at once, serving conflicting maps of safety. Dorn realizes Mardoc's fractured cartography has split the waystation into possibility-space: one version that shelters refugees, one that serves the Tower's hunger. He can maintain only one. Choosing means abandoning hundreds of people to the other version's fate. When a transformed merchant appears offering to *reconcile* the waystation through harmonic song, Dorn suspects the offer is genuine—but at the cost of merging refugees' contradictory memories into a single, coherent identity that may erase who they were. He must broker a deal with Liv's archive to preserve testimonies before they're unmade, or accept that some lives are incompatible with survival.

stories.title: The Auditor's Phantom Archive
stories.text: Sevin the Auditor discovers that her counter-documentation strategy has awakened something in the hidden scriptoriums—a *phantom archivist* who writes records of events that haven't happened yet, using the same hand as the ancient Vault-Keeper. The phantom-entries detail a reversal of the Luminous Ascension occurring within three weeks, but at a cost: the reversal will restore the old stars and erase everyone born after the Ascension. Sevin must decide whether to suppress this knowledge (and potentially doom the cycle to repeat) or release it and trigger riots among those facing erasure. Worse, the phantom archivist reveals that Sevin herself appears in entries describing her own failure—she's already defeated in those future-ledgers. The question becomes: can counter-documentation change a fate that's already been written into existence, or is she merely documenting her own inevitability?

stories.title: The Deepkin Ambassador's Divided Loyalty
stories.text: Kaela, a deepkin captain chosen to renegotiate the covenant, surfaces at Vel-Karsh harbor with disturbing news: the Council of Depths has fractured. A faction led by Krell the Ironbound advocates for immediate flooding of the surface, before Meren's curse shatters and the anchor fails. Kaela is ordered to sabotage the negotiations from within, to ensure the flooding begins. But she's begun to doubt—she's seen transformed merchants who remember pre-ascension songs, refugees who carry the deepkin's own stolen histories. When she encounters Kess briefly at a waystation, Kess speaks of *choosing* the surface world over the depths. Kaela must choose: complete her sabotage and doom the surface realms, or defect and face execution by the Council—knowing that either choice may trigger the catastrophe anyway. Her loyalty is torn between duty and conscience.

stories.title: The Merchant's Harmonic Hunger
stories.text: Lysander, a transformed merchant half-luminous since the Ascension, discovers that his body is slowly becoming *host* to the Tower's harmonic frequency. Each day, he sings slightly higher, and the Singing Scar—the boundary between transformed and human—draws closer to populated settlements. He seeks out other transformed beings at the Sunken Cathedrals, hoping to understand the hunger that pulls them toward merger with the Tower. There he meets Raen, a spice merchant whose transformation is progressing differently—she's *fading*, becoming translucent, as if she's being consumed from the inside. Together they realize the transformed merchants are not all ascending equally; some are being recycled by the Tower, unmade back into component frequencies. Lysander begins a desperate search for a way to transform *backward*, but every half-measures he attempts only accelerates the process. He's given a choice by the Wanderer itself: complete the transformation willingly, and perhaps influence the Tower's mercy toward others, or resist and watch his consciousness fragment across all the others who've already been consumed.

stories.title: The Deepkin Warden's Last Treaty
stories.text: Sylas, the ancient deepkin warden, convenes what may be the final session of the Council of Depths. Meren's curse is destabilizing faster than predicted—the boundary anchor is developing *cracks*, and through them, surface pressure is slowly inverting the seal. Sylas proposes a radical counter-working: sacrifice the covenant entirely and seal the boundary from the *inside*, trapping the deepkin forever but preventing surface-flooding. But several council members propose an alternative—accelerate Meren's curse-breaking and renegotiate with a new anchor-bearer, one willing to accept deeper prices. A transformed merchant arrives with a third option: if deepkin and surface-dwellers sing the Drowned God's name in harmony, the boundary might rewrite itself into something neither sealed nor flooded, but genuinely shared. Sylas must weigh ancient laws against survival, knowing that whichever path the Council chooses, the age of the deepkin as a hidden civilization is ending. The real question is whether they end in dignity or in desperate compromise.
